catastrophic regime collapse. Total 2024 estimate approximately 15,000-25,000
including offensive. Severe violence in 2024.

2025.assessment:
Post-Assad transition. Continued violence from factional competition — SDF vs
Turkish-backed SNA clashes, IS attacks, Alawi coastal unrest, Druze militias.
HTS security operations. Estimated 3,000-7,000 killed in 2025. High but
declining from 2024 offensive. Moderate-to-high violence.

2009.assessment:
Assad government controlled all of Syria. No armed opposition, no contested
territory. Full authoritarian control with security services pervasive throughout.

2010.assessment:
Assad government controlled all of Syria. Pre-Arab Spring, no armed challenge.
Full authoritarian control.

2011.assessment:
Civil uprising began March 2011. Assad government controlled most territory.
Protests spread nationally but military-security retained control. Some areas
in Deraa, Homs, and suburbs of Damascus became contested as armed uprising
emerged late 2011. Government controlled approximately 90% at year end.

2012.assessment:
Civil war escalating rapidly. Government lost control of large areas — rural
Idlib, parts of Aleppo, Deir ez-Zor, Raqqa governorate. Fighting in Damascus
suburbs. Government controlled major cities and strategic areas. FSA and other
groups held significant territory. Government approximately 60-70% territory.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 2012.

2013.assessment:
Civil war intensifying. Government lost significant territory in north and east.
Raqqa fell to opposition (March 2013). ISIS/ISIL emerged. Government controlled
major cities (Damascus, Latakia, Homs partially, Hama), Alawi heartland coast.
Opposition and ISIS controlled much of north and east. Government approximately
50% territory.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 'A/HRC/25/65.' 2014.

2014.assessment:
ISIS declared Caliphate June 2014 and controlled large eastern Syria. Government
controlled approximately 20-35% territory — Damascus and surroundings, Latakia,
Tartous coast, Hama, parts of Homs. ISIS controlled east (Raqqa, Deir ez-Zor),
opposition controlled Idlib and Aleppo areas. Multi-actor fragmentation.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Syria's Metastasising Conflicts.' Middle East Report No. 143, 2014.

2015.assessment:
Assad regime territory at minimum — approximately 17-22%. Russia intervened
September 2015 to prevent further collapse. ISIS controlled massive eastern
territory. SDF/YPG controlling northeast. HTS/opposition in northwest (Idlib,
Aleppo) and parts of south. Government retained Damascus, Latakia, coast,
Hama, parts of Homs. Multiple competing authorities — failed state conditions.

sources.citation: ICG. 'Russia's Syrian Gamble.' Middle East Briefing No. 47, 2015.

2016.assessment:
Regime recovering territory with Russian/Iranian support. Aleppo recaptured
December 2016. ISIS losing territory to SDF and regime. Government approximately
30-40% territory. SDF controlled northeast. HTS/opposition in Idlib. Multiple
competing authorities remained.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 'A/HRC/34/64.' 2017.

2017.assessment:
Regime continuing reconquest with Russian support. Deir ez-Zor relieved September
2017. ISIS territorially collapsing. SDF controlling northeast approximately 25%.
Government approximately 50-60%. HTS in Idlib approximately 5-10%. Multiple
authorities still competing.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 2017 reports.

2018.assessment:
Regime controlled approximately 60-70%. Eastern Ghouta February-April 2018.
Daraa/southwest reconquered July 2018. SDF controlled northeast (~25%).
HTS controlled Idlib (~7%). ISIS remnants in desert. Multiple authorities.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 2018 reports.

2019.assessment:
Government controlled approximately 65% of territory. SDF/US northeast approximately
25%. HTS Idlib approximately 7-8%. Turkish-backed operation Euphrates Shield area.
Multiple competing authorities but regime dominant. Idlib offensive began.

2020.assessment:
Government approximately 65-70% after Idlib ceasefire March 2020. Turkish-backed
forces held northwest. SDF northeast. Ceasefire stabilized Idlib but Turkish
military present. Multiple competing authorities persisted.

2021.assessment:
Frozen conflict. Government approximately 63-65%. SDF northeast approximately
25%. Turkish-backed factions northwest approximately 8%. HTS Idlib approximately
7%. Multiple competing authorities in frozen conflict lines.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 'A/HRC/48/70.' 2021.

2022.assessment:
Frozen conflict continuing. Government approximately 65%. SDF/US northeast
approximately 25%. Turkish-backed factions northwest. HTS Idlib. Lines largely
unchanged. Multiple competing authorities.

2023.assessment:
Frozen conflict. Lines largely unchanged. Government approximately 65%. SDF
northeast. Turkish-backed northwest. HTS Idlib. Arab normalization with Assad.
Multiple competing authorities.

2024.assessment:
HTS-led offensive November 27: Aleppo fell November 30, Hama December 5,
Homs December 8, Damascus December 8. Assad fled. By December 8 virtually
all government territory fell to rebel coalition. Post-December: HTS/rebel
coalition controlling most of Syria. SDF retained northeast (~25%).
Turkish-backed factions retained northwest. Multiple competing authorities
in post-Assad transitional period.

2025.assessment:
Post-Assad transitional governance. HTS/Syrian Transitional Government controlling
approximately 55-60% of territory. SDF/US-backed forces controlling northeast
(approximately 25%). Turkish-backed factions controlling northwest. Contested
areas in south (Druze, former regime). Multiple competing authorities in
transitional period.

2009.assessment:
Syria not covered by Open Budget Survey in 2009. Authoritarian Ba'ath state with
limited budget transparency. Budget produced but not publicly available in meaningful
detail. Off-budget security spending significant and completely opaque. No Freedom of
Information law. Scant budget transparency typical of authoritarian Arab states.

2010.assessment:
Syria not included in Open Budget Survey. Authoritarian state with minimal budget
transparency. Budget totals published but detailed line items, military spending,
and off-budget items not disclosed. Pre-uprising period; limited transparency reform.
Scant budget information available to public.

2011.assessment:
Uprising year. Budget transparency already minimal and worsening. Security spending
massively increased off-budget. Emergency measures bypassed normal budget process.
Syria not in Open Budget Survey. Scant transparency deteriorating to no information
as conflict spending dominated.

2012.assessment:
Civil war eliminated most budget transparency. Government functioned only in regime
areas. Budget process nominal — war economy dominated. Russia and Iran providing
off-budget financing. International sanctions disrupted external audit. No information
available to public about government finances.

2013.assessment:
No budget transparency. Civil war conditions. Government budget for regime-controlled
areas only — not national. Major funding through Iranian loans and Russian credit.
Chemical weapons, military spending entirely opaque. No public budget documentation.
No information available.

2014.assessment:
No budget transparency. ISIS controlled eastern territory with separate financing
(oil smuggling revenues). Regime budget opaque. Multiple governance systems with
no unified national budget. No public information on government finances.

2015.assessment:
No budget transparency. Russia's intervention September 2015 involved opaque
financial arrangements. Multiple governance structures with separate finances.
Regime budget opaque. No public information on government finances.

2016.assessment:
No budget transparency. Regime reconquering territory but budget entirely opaque.
Syria not included in Open Budget Survey. SDF/YPG in northeast had separate finances
(US support, local taxation). HTS Idlib separate. No public information on regime
budget. No information available.

2017.assessment:
No budget transparency. Assad regime maintaining budget in controlled areas but
entirely opaque. Syria not in Open Budget Survey. World Bank/IMF not engaged.
Captagon drug trade emerging as revenue source — off budget. No information.

2018.assessment:
No budget transparency. Regime budget covering approximately 65% of territory but
entirely non-transparent. Syria not in Open Budget Survey. Caesar Act pending made
international engagement on budget reform impossible. No information available.

2019.assessment:
No budget transparency. Syria not in Open Budget Survey. Caesar Act signed December
2019. Syrian pound hyperinflating. Government finances in collapse. No public
budget information. Scant or no information.

2020.assessment:
No budget transparency. Caesar Act entered force June 2020. Syrian pound collapse
catastrophic (lost 80%+ value). Government budget in hyperinflationary chaos. No
public information on government finances. Syria not in OBS. No information.

2021.assessment:
No budget transparency. Syria not in Open Budget Survey. Assad sham re-election
May 2021 had no impact on fiscal transparency. Government budget in nominal terms
only — actual finances through wartime informal channels. No information.

2022.assessment:
No budget transparency. Syria not in Open Budget Survey. Arab normalization beginning
did not include budget transparency reform. Government finances entirely opaque.
Captagon trade major revenue — off-budget entirely. No information.

2023.assessment:
No budget transparency. Syria not in Open Budget Survey. Arab League readmission
no transparency improvement. Earthquake humanitarian response through international
aid channels — not through government budget. No public information on government
finances.

2024.assessment:
No budget transparency. Assad regime budget opaque through regime collapse December 8.
Post-December 8: Syrian Transitional Government beginning to form budget processes
from scratch. International engagement (IMF, World Bank discussions) beginning.
2024 budget data unavailable. Transitioning from no information toward scant.

2025.assessment:
Early transitional budget formation. Syrian Transitional Government beginning
to establish Ministry of Finance capacity. International support for public financial
management reform sought. Actual transparency still very limited — scant at best.
Syria not yet in Open Budget Survey. Gulf states and IMF providing initial fiscal
support with some conditions. Scant budget transparency.

2009.assessment:
Syria is an oil-producing state with declining production. Not a member of EITI
(Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative). Ba'ath authoritarian state kept
oil and gas revenues completely opaque. Syrian Petroleum Company (state-owned)
reported to government but no public disclosure. No extractive transparency.
No membership, minimal disclosure.

2010.assessment:
Syria not EITI member. Oil production approximately 380,000 barrels/day (declining
from peak of 600,000 in mid-1990s). Revenue opaque — major budget source but
not publicly disclosed. No extractive transparency. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2011.assessment:
Syria not EITI member. Oil production disrupted by uprising. EU oil embargo
announced December 2011 (effective February 2012). Syria's oil revenues began
collapsing. Extractive revenues entirely opaque. No disclosure. No membership,
minimal disclosure.

2012.assessment:
Syria not EITI member. EU oil embargo (effective February 2012) eliminated most
oil exports. Production collapsed in conflict areas. Oil fields in east seized by
opposition/ISIS. Remaining production opaque. Extractive revenues minimal and
entirely non-transparent. No membership, no disclosure.

2013.assessment:
Syria not EITI member. ISIS beginning to control oil fields in eastern Syria
(Raqqa, Deir ez-Zor area). ISIS oil revenues funding operations — entirely opaque.
Regime oil production minimal. No extractive transparency from any actor.
No membership, no disclosure.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts on ISIL. 'ISIS oil revenues.' 2015.

2014.assessment:
ISIS controlling major oil fields generating $1-3 million/day in oil revenues (UN
estimates). Regime retained minimal oil production. SDF/YPG later controlling some
fields. Multiple actors with opaque oil revenues. No extractive transparency from
any actor. No disclosure.

sources.citation: UN Security Council. 'ISIL financing from oil.' S/2015/358. 2015.

2015.assessment:
ISIS oil revenues targeted by US/Russian coalition airstrikes. ISIS oil revenues
declining from peak. SDF gaining some oil fields in northeast. Regime minimal oil.
Russian military intervention began September 2015 targeting ISIS oil infrastructure.
All extractive revenues opaque. No disclosure.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense. 'Operation Tidal Wave II — ISIS oil.' 2015.

2016.assessment:
ISIS oil revenues largely destroyed by airstrikes. SDF controlling most Syrian
oil (northeast fields). Regime minimal oil. Russian and Iranian economic deals
for post-war oil concessions opaque. No EITI. No extractive transparency.
No disclosure, opaque.

2017.assessment:
SDF (US-backed) controlling northeast oil fields (~90,000 bbl/day capacity, much
damaged). Regime minimal oil. Russian company Stroytransgaz receiving phosphate
concessions. Syrian government contracted Russian/Iranian companies for post-war
oil. No extractive transparency from any actor. No disclosure.

2018.assessment:
SDF still controlling northeast oil under US oversight (US company — Rosneft/Delta
Crescent Energy discussions). Regime controlling minimal oil. Russian/Iranian
concessional deals. No EITI. All extractive revenues opaque. No disclosure.

2019.assessment:
SDF controlling northeast oil. US company Delta Crescent Energy contract with SDF
for northeast oil development (2020). Regime minimal oil. No extractive transparency.
Caesar Act signed December 2019 — any oil revenues subject to secondary sanctions.
No disclosure.

sources.citation: US Department of State. 'Syria oil — SDF.' 2020.

2020.assessment:
Caesar Act June 2020 targeted oil revenues. SDF/Autonomous Administration of
Northeast Syria controlling oil with some US oversight (Delta Crescent Energy
contract). Regime receiving minimal oil revenues. All opaque. No extractive
transparency. No disclosure, opaque.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC. 'Caesar Act oil provisions.' June 2020.

2021.assessment:
SDF/AANES controlling northeast oil with US backing. Regime minimal oil production.
Captagon drug trade becoming major regime revenue — completely opaque and not
extractive. Syria not EITI. All oil revenues opaque. No disclosure.

2022.assessment:
SDF/AANES controlling northeast oil (~80,000 bbl/day estimated). Regime Captagon
drug revenues (estimated $5+ billion annually). Syria not EITI. All extractive
and quasi-extractive revenues completely opaque. No disclosure.

2023.assessment:
Continued opaque extractive revenues. AANES northeast oil production. Assad Captagon
revenues. Arab League readmission no impact on extractive transparency. No EITI.
No disclosure. The Captagon trade exposed Assad regime's criminal economy but no
reform of transparency.

2024.assessment:
Assad regime collapse December 8. Captagon trade infrastructure disrupted by
collapse. AANES northeast oil continuing under SDF. Post-collapse: HTS/transitional
government inheriting resource transparency vacuum. Early discussions of economic
reconstruction and resource management transparency. No EITI membership. No disclosure.

2025.assessment:
Transitional government beginning discussions on resource management. Syria's oil
resources (northeastern fields, phosphates) under multiple actors (AANES, Turkish
backed groups, new government). No EITI membership or application initiated yet.
International reconstruction discussions noting need for extractive transparency.
Minimal disclosure only — early transitional. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2009.assessment:
Syria under authoritarian Ba'ath Party rule. No WJP Rule of Law Index data for
Syria in 2009. Legal system nominally existed — civil codes, commercial law,
criminal code — but courts were controlled by executive and security services.
Emergency Law (since 1963) suspended normal legal rights. Security courts operated
without due process. Laws not freely accessible. Difficult and arbitrary enforcement.

sources.citation: US State Department. 'Syria 2009 Human Rights Report.' state.gov.

2010.assessment:
No WJP data for Syria. Authoritarian legal system with Emergency Law in force.
Commercial law functioned adequately for business (limited liberalization). Criminal
law arbitrarily applied. Courts not independent. Difficult and arbitrary enforcement
of laws, particularly for political matters. Limited legal transparency.

sources.citation: US State Department. 'Syria 2010 Human Rights Report.' state.gov.

2011.assessment:
Legal framework collapsing with uprising. Emergency Law lifted April 2011 but
replaced by terror law with equivalent powers. Security crackdown operated outside
legal frameworks. Mass arbitrary detentions without trial. Courts suspended
in conflict areas. Minimal rule by decree in crisis mode. Difficult to minimal
legal transparency.

sources.citation: Amnesty International. 'Syria: Human Rights Crisis 2011.' amnesty.org.

2012.assessment:
Legal framework near-collapsed. Civil war conditions. Courts operating only in
regime strongholds with no independence. Military courts and security services
acting without legal constraint. Opposition areas had no formal legal framework.
ISIS establishing sharia courts in captured areas. No functioning legal framework
at national level. Minimal to no accessible framework.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 2012.

2013.assessment:
Legal framework collapsed nationally. Multiple competing legal systems: Ba'ath
security law in regime areas, ISIS sharia courts in east, opposition local councils
with improvised legal arrangements. No rule of law. Chemical weapons use violated
international law without accountability. No accessible legal framework nationally.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 'A/HRC/25/65.' 2014.

2014.assessment:
No accessible legal framework nationally. ISIS imposed brutal sharia law in
caliphate territory. Regime security courts. Kurdish Rojava establishing own
legal framework (communal justice). Multiple competing legal systems with
no national rule of law. No accessible framework.

2015.assessment:
No accessible national legal framework. Multiple competing legal systems persisted.
ISIS caliphate sharia dominant in east. Regime security courts in west. SDF/Rojava
in northeast. All systems highly opaque and arbitrary from external perspective.
No published accessible laws serving full population. No accessible framework.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 2015 reports.

2016.assessment:
No accessible legal framework nationally. Regime retaking territory and imposing
Ba'ath security law in reconquered areas. ISIS governance collapsing. SDF
northeast. HTS Idlib. All arbitrary and non-accessible to public. No accessible
framework nationally.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 'A/HRC/34/64.' 2017.

2017.assessment:
No accessible legal framework. Regime controlled ~55% with Ba'ath security
courts (no independence, no transparency). SDF northeast Rojava communal law.
HTS Idlib sharia. ISIS defeated territorially but areas in chaos. No national
accessible legal framework.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 2017 reports.

2018.assessment:
No accessible legal framework nationally. Regime Ba'ath courts in ~65% territory
— not independent, not accessible. SDF northeast maintained own legal system.
HTS Idlib sharia courts. Minimal to no accessible framework depending on zone.
Caesar Act pending highlighted legal barriers to reconstruction. No accessible nationally.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Syria. 'A/HRC/39/65.' 2018.

2019.assessment:
No accessible legal framework. Frozen conflict with entrenched parallel legal
systems. Caesar Act signed December 2019 — international legal pressure for
accountability. Property law Decree 66 and Law 10 enabled regime confiscation
of displaced persons' property (2018). No accessible transparent legal framework
nationally.

2020.assessment:
No accessible legal framework nationally. Caesar Act June 2020 imposed international
sanctions on Syrian legal/financial actors. Regime courts continued operating
without independence. Caesar Act prosecution potential for Syrian judges/lawyers
enabling regime. SDF northeast maintained Rojava courts. No accessible framework.

sources.citation: US Treasury. 'Caesar Act — Syria legal framework sanctions.' 2020.

2021.assessment:
No accessible legal framework. Assad sham re-election May 2021 legitimized through
controlled courts. No independent judiciary. SDF northeast maintained own legal
system. HTS Idlib sharia. No accessible national framework.

2022.assessment:
No accessible legal framework. WJP Syria not covered. Regime courts in ~65%
territory but no independence or transparency. SDF northeast. HTS Idlib. Frozen
conflict with multiple competing legal systems. No national accessible framework.

2023.assessment:
No accessible legal framework. Arab League readmission did not improve legal
transparency. Earthquake further overwhelmed whatever legal administration remained.
WJP not covering Syria. No accessible framework.

2024.assessment:
Assad regime legal system collapsed December 8. Courts, security courts, and
legal framework dissolved. Post-collapse: HTS/transitional government announcing
formation of new legal frameworks. Syrian Transitional Government inheriting legal
vacuum. Early 2025 constitution discussions. No accessible framework in transition.

2025.assessment:
Legal framework rebuilding from near-zero. Syrian Transitional Government forming
court structures. HTS legal traditions (Islamist) influencing new framework.
SDF northeast maintained separate Rojava legal system. Constitutional process
beginning but incomplete. Minimal rule by decree/announcement in transitional
period. Early-stage reconstruction with significant gaps and opacity.

2009.assessment:
Syria among world's worst for press freedom. RSF 2009 score: 7.44/100 (raw, where
lower = worse; high inverted score = more repression). Ranked near bottom globally.
No independent media. State controls all TV, radio, major newspapers. Foreign
journalists tightly restricted. Internet censorship pervasive. Bloggers arrested.
Al-Monitor, Al-Arabiya blocked. No press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2009.' rsf.org.

2010.assessment:
Syria near bottom globally for press freedom. RSF 2010: approximately 6.94/100 raw.
No independent print, broadcast, or online media permitted. State owns all major
media. Journalists imprisoned for criticizing government. Internet filtering blocking
social media and news sites. No press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2010.' rsf.org.

2011.assessment:
Press freedom catastrophically worsened with uprising. Journalists and citizen
journalists killed covering protests. Foreign media expelled. Internet/social media
blocked intermittently. Citizen journalism via smartphone videos became primary
documentation. RSF ~6.48/100 raw. Among global worst. No press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2011-2012.' rsf.org.

2012.assessment:
Syria one of world's most dangerous for journalists. RSF ~6.08/100 raw. Journalists
from multiple foreign outlets killed (Marie Colvin, Remi Ochlik, February 2012 Homs).
Citizen journalists targeted by snipers. No independent media in regime areas.
Opposition areas had underground media. No press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2013.' rsf.org.
CPJ. 'Syria: Most Dangerous Country for Journalists 2012.' cpj.org.

2013.assessment:
World's most dangerous country for journalists. RSF ~5.36/100 raw. ISIS began
kidnapping foreign journalists (James Foley captured November 2012, executed 2014).
Regime targeting journalists. No press freedom in any governance area. Multiple
foreign journalists kidnapped by various armed groups. No press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2014.' rsf.org.

2014.assessment:
Syria at absolute bottom of global press freedom. RSF ~4.87/100 raw. James Foley
and Steven Sotloff executed by ISIS on video August/September 2014. ISIS imposed
media ban in controlled areas — only ISIS propaganda. Regime areas state media only.
No press freedom anywhere in Syria.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2015.' rsf.org.
CPJ. 'James Foley/Steven Sotloff execution by ISIS.' 2014.

2015.assessment:
Syria at global bottom. RSF ~4.83/100 raw — world's worst or near-worst. ISIS
propaganda apparatus (Dabiq magazine, videos) only media in caliphate. Regime
state media. SDF areas limited independent media. No press freedom anywhere.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2016.' rsf.org.

2016.assessment:
Continued at global worst. RSF ~4.79/100 raw. Aleppo journalists and citizen
journalists killed during siege. White Helmets documenting attacks as volunteer
first responders under attack. No independent journalism in regime or ISIS areas.
No press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2017.' rsf.org.

2017.assessment:
Syria ranked near worst globally. RSF ~4.76/100 raw. ISIS territorial defeat
removing one source of media repression, but regime media control in reconquered
areas remained total. HTS Idlib restrictions emerging. No press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2018.' rsf.org.

2018.assessment:
Continued near-worst globally. RSF ~4.78/100 raw. Regime controlled approximately
65% of territory — all state media. HTS Idlib imposed media restrictions. SDF
northeast had limited independent press. No press freedom at national level.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2019.' rsf.org.

2019.assessment:
Syria ranked near worst globally. RSF ~4.38/100 raw. Frozen conflict with entrenched
media repression. Regime state media dominant. HTS Idlib restricting independent
press. SDF northeast had some independent media. Overall national: no press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2020.' rsf.org.

2020.assessment:
Syria near global worst. RSF ~4.36/100 raw. Continued entrenched media repression.
COVID-19 used as pretext for additional media restrictions. Regime state media only.
HTS Idlib restrictions. No independent journalism in regime areas. No press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2021.' rsf.org.

2021.assessment:
Syria near bottom globally. RSF ~4.37/100 raw. Assad sham re-election May 2021
— state media dominated coverage, no independent coverage permitted. Continued
arbitrary detention of journalists. Frozen conflict media repression entrenched.
No press freedom nationally.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2022.' rsf.org.

2022.assessment:
Syria at global worst. RSF 2023 Syria score approximately 4.29/100 raw (world's
second worst after North Korea in 2022 RSF ranking, or near bottom). Entrenched
media repression. Arab normalization not affecting media freedom. No press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2023.' rsf.org.

2023.assessment:
Syria near global worst. RSF 2024 Syria score approximately 4.33/100 raw. Arab
League readmission no improvement. Earthquake further disrupted independent media.
Continued systematic repression of journalism. No press freedom.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2024.' rsf.org.

2024.assessment:
Syria RSF 2024 score ~4.38/100 raw (covering pre-December period). Post-December 8
Assad collapse: HTS/transitional government initially signaled more press freedom.
Some independent media began operating in Damascus. HTS propaganda apparatus existing.
RSF score reflects pre-collapse period. Post-collapse uncertain improvement. Transition
from no press freedom toward very serious restrictions.

sources.citation: Reporters Without Borders. 'Press Freedom Index 2024.' rsf.org.

2025.assessment:
Post-Assad transitional press environment. Some improvement from total state
control but HTS governance retains significant restrictions. Independent media
beginning to operate in Damascus and major cities. HTS has own media apparatus.
Journalists covering transitional government with some restrictions. Women
journalists face additional constraints under HTS social codes. Very serious
restrictions persist but improvement from total repression.

2009.assessment:
Syria Central Bureau of Statistics (CBS) functional in pre-conflict period.
World Bank SCI approximately 56.67/100 in 2009. Syria produced periodic censuses,
national accounts, and household surveys. Some data gaps in security-related
statistics. Moderate statistical capacity — functioning CBS producing reasonably
regular data. Moderate statistical transparency.

2010.assessment:
Syria CBS operating normally. WB SCI approximately 58.89/100. Regular statistical
releases on GDP, prices, trade. Pre-uprising period peak for Syrian statistical
capacity. Moderate statistical capacity — one of better Arab state statistical
systems.

2011.assessment:
CBS continuing to operate in regime-controlled areas. WB SCI ~58.89. Statistical
capacity deteriorating as civil conflict began and international cooperation reduced.
Data quality declining but infrastructure remaining. Moderate statistical capacity
but deteriorating.

2012.assessment:
Statistical capacity sharply declining. Civil war disrupted CBS operations.
WB SCI declined to approximately 46.67. Field surveys impossible in conflict
zones. Census population data increasingly unreliable with displacement.
International statistical cooperation reduced. Moderate declining toward poor.

2013.assessment:
Statistical capacity severely degraded. WB SCI approximately 34.44. CBS functioning
only in regime-held Damascus and major cities. Data from approximately 50% of
territory unavailable. GDP estimates highly unreliable. Population data outdated
with massive displacement. Poor statistical transparency.

2014.assessment:
Statistical system near collapse. WB SCI approximately 28.89. CBS operations
severely limited. Multiple governance zones (regime, ISIS, opposition, SDF) meant
no national statistics possible. GDP, population, unemployment data all unreliable
estimates. Poor statistical transparency.

2015.assessment:
Statistical system at near-bottom. WB SCI approximately 25.56. CBS nominal
operations in Damascus only. No national coverage. GDP estimated only — some
World Bank models showing 50%+ contraction. Massive uncertainty in all statistics.
Very poor to no statistical system.

2016.assessment:
Statistical system near bottom. WB SCI approximately 23.33. CBS barely functioning.
Aleppo recapture some improvement in data coverage. Multiple governance zones
meant no comprehensive national data. Very poor statistical capacity.

2017.assessment:
Slight improvement as regime reconquered territory. WB SCI approximately 25.56.
CBS re-establishing operations in formerly contested areas. Still very limited
coverage. Data from SDF-controlled northeast and HTS Idlib unavailable. Very poor
statistical capacity nationally.

2018.assessment:
Marginal improvement as regime controlled ~65% of territory. WB SCI approximately
27.78. CBS operating in regime areas. Data from northeast (SDF, ~25%) and Idlib
(HTS, ~7%) missing. Still very poor statistical capacity at national level.

2019.assessment:
Marginal improvement in frozen conflict. WB SCI approximately 30. CBS in regime
areas with some data production. GDP models suggest ~50% of pre-war level.
Frozen conflict stabilizing data collection partially. Still poor statistical
capacity — national coverage incomplete.

2020.assessment:
Statistical capacity declining again. WB SCI approximately 28.89. Caesar Act June
2020 disrupted financial systems further. Lebanon banking collapse August 2020
cut off informal data channels. COVID-19 disrupted CBS operations. Very poor
statistical capacity.

2021.assessment:
Very poor statistical capacity. WB SCI approximately 27.78 (2021 — one of last
available before WB discontinued this specific series). CBS nominal operations
only. No IMF Article IV consultations. GDP estimates highly uncertain. Very poor.

2022.assessment:
No WB SCI data (series discontinued). CBS nominal operations in regime areas.
No national statistical coverage. Very poor to no statistical system functioning
meaningfully. Data unreliable for economic decision-making.

2023.assessment:
No WB SCI data available. CBS nominal. Earthquake February 2023 further disrupted
what statistical capacity remained. No reliable national statistics. Very poor
statistical transparency.

2024.assessment:
Assad regime CBS collapsed with regime December 8. Statistical infrastructure
effectively zero in transitional period. Post-collapse: new transitional government
inheriting near-zero statistical capacity. IMF/World Bank initial engagement
discussions. No statistical system effectively functioning.

2025.assessment:
Rebuilding statistical capacity from near-zero. Transitional government began
reconstituting CBS. International organizations (UNDP, World Bank) providing
technical assistance for statistical system rebuilding. No reliable national
statistics yet. Very early stage statistical rebuild. Very poor to no system.
